museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Atari's Star Wars vector cabinet arrives, Ultimate releases Jetpac, Electronic Arts ships its first games, and the home-computer future starts to look less like consolation and more like the main event.
museum.curatorNote: May has several useful anchors, but some computer-game dates are company or year-level rather than day-level. The exhibit marks those as shipped or 1983 context rather than pretending every title hit every shop on one day.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: May brings new evidence that games were not finished with wonder.

items.title: Atari releases Star Wars in arcades
items.body: The colour vector Star Wars cabinet turned trench-run fantasy into a public ritual of yokes, voices and glowing lines.
items.sourceNote: 1983 release summaries list Atari's Star Wars arcade game in May.

items.title: Ultimate Play the Game releases Jetpac
items.body: Jetpac gave the ZX Spectrum a crisp, arcade-like original and introduced the studio that would later become Rare.
items.sourceNote: Ultimate histories list Jetpac in May 1983 for 16K Spectrum.

items.title: Electronic Arts ships its first games
items.body: EA's early software arrived with album-cover packaging and developer-as-artist marketing, including Hard Hat Mack, Archon, M.U.L.E., Worms? and Axis Assassin in the first wave.
items.sourceNote: EA's own retrospective cites May 20, 1983 for first games shipped.

items.title: Computer games gain prestige as console faith fades
items.body: The American console crash did not stop play. It redirected attention toward Apple II, Atari 8-bit, C64 and other home computers.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories and EA first-wave context.

items.title: UK Spectrum culture gets a defining signal
items.body: Jetpac suggested that a British cassette could feel fast, polished and native to its machine rather than like a compromised arcade copy.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight May releases and first-wave objects.

items.title: Star Wars
items.body: Atari's colour vector rail shooter makes the Death Star trench run playable.

items.title: Jetpac
items.body: Ultimate's debut: compact, fast and central to the Spectrum's growing identity.

items.title: Hard Hat Mack
items.body: Part of EA's first shipped wave and a construction-platform game with album-cover swagger.

items.title: Archon: The Light and the Dark
items.body: Chess-like strategy collided with direct action combat in one of EA's signature early releases.
items.sourceNote: EA first-games retrospective and Archon references.

items.title: M.U.L.E.
items.body: A multiplayer economic game that made negotiation, scarcity and spite feel playful.
items.sourceNote: EA first-games retrospective and M.U.L.E. references.

items.title: Axis Assassin
items.body: A tube shooter positioned inside EA's developer-forward launch identity.

items.title: Worms?
items.body: One of EA's first five, less remembered now but part of the company's early experimental shelf.

items.title: Pinball Construction Set
items.body: Often grouped with EA's early 1983 identity, it turned players into builders as much as players.
items.sourceNote: GameDeveloper history places it among EA's spring 1983 products.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: May's objects show why computers looked newly central.

items.title: Atari Star Wars cabinet
items.body: A vector display, flight yoke and film licence turned a cabinet into a cockpit.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 16K/48K
items.body: Jetpac proved that even the smaller Spectrum could carry a fast, original commercial game.

items.title: Atari 8-bit and C64 computers
items.body: EA's first wave leaned into home computers as serious game platforms.

items.title: Apple II
items.body: Still a crucial North American computer-game platform, especially for EA and RPG/strategy audiences.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: May's covers could sell a new kind of authorship.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Arcades meet computer-game auteurs
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for May 1983.
items.curatorNote: EA's developer-led marketing and Atari's Star Wars cabinet both suited magazine storytelling.
items.body: EA's developer-led marketing and Atari's Star Wars cabinet both suited magazine storytelling.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Star Wars, Jetpac and the arcade at home
items.curatorNote: For UK readers, Star Wars was cabinet spectacle; Jetpac was something closer to home.
items.body: For UK readers, Star Wars was cabinet spectacle; Jetpac was something closer to home.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Software houses and serious play
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for May 1983.
items.curatorNote: Home-computer magazines were becoming guides to a real software industry.
items.body: Home-computer magazines were becoming guides to a real software industry.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Jetpac and the 16K arcade dream
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for May 1983.
items.curatorNote: Jetpac made the Spectrum feel less like a cheap computer and more like a games platform with its own stars.
items.body: Jetpac made the Spectrum feel less like a cheap computer and more like a games platform with its own stars.
items.sourceNote: Jetpac release context; specific cover line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: May's network was fandom before feeds.

items.title: Author names mattered
items.body: EA and Ultimate made players notice who made games, not just who sold machines.

items.title: Magazine screenshots sold motion
items.body: Star Wars and Jetpac both needed still images to imply speed.

items.title: Tapes and disks defined tribes
items.body: Your platform decided which software world you could join.

items.title: Word of mouth validated polish
items.body: A game feeling 'arcade quality' was a social claim as much as a review line.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: May felt like an answer to the gloom.

items.title: The arcade could still astonish
items.body: Star Wars made vector lines feel cinematic.

items.title: The Spectrum had a hero game
items.body: Jetpac felt clean, quick and proudly domestic.

items.title: Computer software gained a sleeve culture
items.body: EA boxes treated developers like musicians.

items.title: The future moved sideways
items.body: Not back to old consoles, but toward computers, creators and cassettes.
